text: The surgeon transects the gastrohepatic ligament, exposing the right cruse. Using a harmonic scalpel, the peritoneum on the inferior aspect of the right cruse is transected. A retroesophageal tunnel is created with gentle dissection, carefully protecting the esophagus, and the posterior vagus nerve is observed.
The surgeon begins the dissection by transecting the gastrohepatic ligament, subsequently exposing the right crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the peritoneum at the inferior aspect of the right cruse, creating a retroesophageal tunnel while carefully protecting the esophagus during dissection.
The surgeon identifies the posterior vagus nerve and carefully dissects it out, exposing approximately three to four centimeters of the nerve.

The surgeon carefully dissects the posterior vagus nerve, exposing it for three to four centimeters. Clips are placed on either side of the nerve, and the harmonic scalpel is used to transect the nerve between the clips.
Clips are placed on either side of the posterior vagus nerve. The harmonic scalpel is then used to transect the posterior vagus nerve between the clips.

Following the transection of the posterior vagus nerve, the surgeon proceeds with dissection anteriorly to the esophagus, transecting the phrenoesophageal ligament. The left crus is exposed, along with the anterior aspect of the esophagus.

Following the transection of the posterior vagus nerve, the surgeon proceeds with dissection anteriorly to the esophagus, transecting the phrenoesophageal ligament. The left crus and the anterior aspect of the esophagus are exposed.

The anterior vagus nerve is visible extending from the left aspect of the mediastinum to the central portion over the esophagus. The surgeon gently separates the nerve from the anterior portion of the esophagus, places clips proximally and distally, and uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the nerve between the clips.

The anterior vagus nerve is identified extending from the left aspect of the mediastinum to the central portion over the esophagus. The surgeon gently separates the nerve from the anterior portion of the esophagus.
Clips are placed proximally and distally on the nerve, and the harmonic scalpel is used to transect the nerve between the clips.
The surgeon closes the hiatus, visually approximating the tissue edges to secure the opening.
The surgeon closes the hiatus, visually approximating the tissue edges to secure the opening.

Following the completion of the vagotomy, the surgeon opens the lesser sac to prepare for the gastrojejunostomy. The posterior wall of the stomach and the ligament of Treitz are identified. The small bowel is measured approximately fifty to sixty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. A stay suture is placed between the loop of jejunum and the posterior wall of the stomach, and enterotomies are made using the harmonic scalpel.

Following the completion of the vagotomy, the surgeon opens the lesser sac to prepare for the gastrojejunostomy. The posterior wall of the stomach is identified, along with the ligament of Treitz. The small bowel is measured approximately fifty to sixty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz.
A stay suture is placed between the loop of jejunum and the posterior wall of the stomach. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make enterotomies in preparation for the gastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon creates the gastrojejunostomy using a 60mm blue load stapler, joining the loop of jejunum to the posterior wall of the stomach. The resulting defect is closed in two layers with 2-0 Vicryl sutures.
The surgeon creates the gastrojejunostomy using a 60mm blue load stapler, joining the stomach and jejunum. The resulting defect is closed in two layers with 2-0 Vicryl sutures.
